era_context: The Crisis of the Third Century is at its height. The Roman Empire is fragmented into three competing power centers: the central state under Gallienus, the Gallic Empire in the west, and the Palmyrene Empire in the east. Gallienus is struggling to maintain control against military threats and internal challenges. In China, the Three Kingdoms period is in its final phase, with the Jin Dynasty preparing for unification. The Sassanid Persian Empire is stable and expanding.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier accounts

events.title: Death of Odaenathus and succession of Zenobia as regent
events.description: Odaenathus, the Palmyrene king who had risen to prominence as a Roman ally against the Sassanids, was assassinated in 267 CE. The exact circumstances of his death are unclear, but he was succeeded by his widow Zenobia, who assumed regency for their young son Vaballathus. Zenobia's assumption of power marked a turning point: she would transform Palmyra from a Roman client state into an independent empire. Her intelligence, political acumen, and ambition would make her one of the most remarkable figures of the third century.
events.certainty_note: Odaenathus' death and Zenobia's regency are well-attested by numismatic and epigraphic evidence. The exact date (267 CE) and circumstances of his death are less certain.

events.title: Gallienus continues to struggle against multiple threats
events.description: In 267 CE, Gallienus (r. 253–268 CE) continued to face multiple military and political challenges. The Goths and other Germanic tribes threatened the Danube frontier. The Gallic Empire controlled the western provinces. The Palmyrene Empire was expanding in the east. Gallienus' reign is traditionally viewed as a period of decline and military failure, though modern scholars debate the extent of his actual incompetence. His military reforms, including the creation of a mobile cavalry force, were significant innovations.
events.certainty_note: Gallienus' difficulties in 267 CE are attested by multiple sources, but the specific events and their significance are debated.

events.title: Gallic Empire maintains control of western provinces
events.description: The Gallic Empire, established in 260 CE, continued to function as an independent state in 267 CE. The exact ruler is debated (possibly Postumus or his successor), but the Gallic Empire maintained control over Gaul, Britannia, and Hispania. The Gallic Empire minted coins, maintained an army, and administered the western provinces as a separate political entity, defending against Germanic incursions.
key_figures: Postumus or successor (emperor of Gallic Empire)
events.certainty_note: The existence of the Gallic Empire in 267 CE is secure, but the exact ruler and specific events are uncertain.

events.title: Western Jin Dynasty consolidates power in China
events.description: The Western Jin Dynasty was consolidating power in 267 CE. Emperor Wu (Sima Yan) was establishing administrative structures and preparing for the final conquest of the southern kingdoms. The period was marked by relative stability and military preparation for the unification campaign that would occur in 280 CE.
events.certainty_note: The Western Jin Dynasty's existence and general chronology are well-established in Chinese historical records.

events.title: Sassanid Persia under Shapur I or successor
events.description: The Sassanid Persian Empire remained stable and powerful in 267 CE. The exact ruler is debated (possibly Shapur I, Hormizd I, or Bahram I, depending on chronological system), but the empire maintained control over its territories and benefited from Rome's internal crisis. The Sassanid state was one of the few stable great powers during this period.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: The Sassanid Empire's stability is clear, but the exact ruler and specific events of 267 CE are uncertain due to disputed chronologies.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Odaenathus as a loyal Roman ally is complicated by evidence suggesting he may have pursued independent ambitions. The exact circumstances of his death (assassination vs. natural causes) are debated. The characterization of Gallienus as an incompetent ruler is challenged by modern scholars who emphasize his military innovations and the structural problems he inherited. The Gallic Empire's exact chronology and succession is disputed; Historia Augusta is unreliable for this period.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 267 CE is heavily dependent on later compilations, particularly Historia Augusta, which is known to be unreliable. Contemporary sources are sparse: numismatic evidence (coins) and epigraphic evidence (inscriptions) provide the most reliable data. The narrative is heavily weighted toward the Roman perspective. Chinese sources for this period are more reliable and detailed. The Sassanid record is fragmentary. The overall picture is one of imperial fragmentation and military crisis, but specific events and their dating are often uncertain.

graph_edges.note: Odaenathus' death and Zenobia's regency led to Palmyrene expansion
Gallienus' struggles in 267 CE led to his death in 268 CE
